message:
add startStream() API; rewrite docs in honest tone

Two changes that share a commit because the docs sweep was prompted
by the streaming work being added:

DetectStream API (textsift-core):
  filter.startStream(opts) → DetectStreamSession with append() and
  finish(). Each append() runs the model on a trailing window of the
  accumulated buffer and yields newly-stable spans (those whose end
  is more than safetyMarginTokens behind the trailing edge). finish()
  flushes via the full chunked pipeline. Total work over a stream of
  N tokens is O(N), versus O(N²) for the naive pattern of calling
  detect(buffer) after every chunk arrives. Designed for AI-proxy /
  LLM-output-filtering: stream LLM tokens directly into append(),
  short-circuit on high-confidence span detection.

  New file: packages/textsift-core/src/js/inference/stream.ts.
  New test: tests/browser/stream.spec.ts verifies streaming yields
  the same span set as a single batch detect() call on the
  reassembled text. 6/6 spans match.

Docs honesty pass:
  Removed marketing language across README, docs-site (index,
  benchmarks, backends, architecture), and per-package READMEs.
  Specific changes:
    - "10× faster than tjs" → "default-configuration comparison;
      tjs can match this if you wire up an OPFS cache"
    - "the only package that runs..." → "currently the only working
      WASM path for this model" (factual, scoped to ORT-Web's missing
      kernels)
    - Bundle sizes corrected to measured numbers (76 KB, 226 KB)
    - Cold-start table footnoted to clarify it's defaults vs defaults
    - Architecture page updated to reflect the textsift-core /
      textsift split and the native tokenizer (no longer
      AutoTokenizer)
    - Index hero retitled "What this is" with five concrete claims
      about packaging value, removing the "why we matter" pitch

files.patch:
@@ -1,25 +1,25 @@
 # textsift
 
-**PII detection and redaction for the browser, Node, and edge runtimes — powered by `openai/privacy-filter`.**
-
-Runs entirely on the user's device. WebGPU on capable browsers, WebAssembly + SIMD128 everywhere else. No backend, no network calls to inference services, no text ever leaves the device.
+PII detection and redaction that runs [openai/privacy-filter](https://huggingface.co/openai/privacy-filter) on the user's device. WebGPU when available; Zig + SIMD128 WASM otherwise. Apache 2.0.
 
 [**Docs**](https://teamchong.github.io/textsift/) · [**Quickstart**](https://teamchong.github.io/textsift/quickstart/) · [**Playground**](https://teamchong.github.io/textsift/playground/) · [**API**](https://teamchong.github.io/textsift/api/)
 
-## Two packages
+## What this is
+
+A npm package. Two flavours, same `PrivacyFilter` API:
 
 ```sh
-# Lean: 76 KB gzipped, native o200k-style BPE tokenizer, no transformers.js dep.
-# Recommended for browser / edge / proxy apps that want the smallest bundle.
-npm install textsift-core
-
-# Full: 226 KB gzipped, adds a transformers.js fallback for runtimes
-# without WebGPU and without SIMD-capable WASM. Drop-in for the
-# old single-package install.
-npm install textsift
+npm install textsift-core   # 76 KB gzipped, no runtime deps
+npm install textsift        # 226 KB gzipped, adds a transformers.js fallback backend
 ```
 
-Both expose the same `PrivacyFilter` API. Same model, same spans, same docs.
+The model is OpenAI's; the value here is packaging:
+
+- A native o200k-style BPE tokenizer in pure TypeScript (no `@huggingface/transformers` dep on `textsift-core`).
+- Two custom backends — WGSL for WebGPU and Zig-compiled SIMD WASM — that produce byte-identical span output to transformers.js's WebGPU path.
+- A WASM path that loads `model_q4f16.onnx` at all. transformers.js's WASM EP doesn't, because ORT-Web's WASM bundle has no `MatMulNBits` / `GatherBlockQuantized` kernel.
+- Persistent OPFS caching of the 770 MB model weights, configured by default.
+- An incremental `startStream()` API for AI-proxy / LLM-output-filtering use cases. O(N) over a stream of N tokens, vs O(N²) for the naive `detect(buffer)` pattern.
 
 ## Use
 
@@ -31,7 +31,6 @@ const filter = await PrivacyFilter.create();
 const result = await filter.redact(
   "Hi, my name is John Smith and my email is john@example.com.",
 );
-
 // result.redactedText
 //   "Hi, my name is [private_person] and my email is [private_email]."
 
@@ -40,52 +39,48 @@ const result = await filter.redact(
 //     { label: "private_email",  start: 43, end: 59, ... } ]
 ```
 
-Detection without applying redactions:
+Detect-only:
 
 ```ts
 const { spans, containsPii } = await filter.detect(text);
 ```
 
-Batch inputs, custom markers, per-category enabling — see the [API reference](https://teamchong.github.io/textsift/api/).
-
-## Why
+Streaming detect (proxy use case):
 
-OpenAI released [`openai/privacy-filter`](https://huggingface.co/openai/privacy-filter) on 2026-04-20 — a 1.5B-parameter MoE (50M active) bidirectional token classifier for PII detection. Apache 2.0. State-of-the-art on PII-Masking-300k (96% F1).
+```ts
+const session = await filter.startStream();
+for (const llmChunk of llmStream) {
+  for await (const span of session.append(llmChunk)) {
+    if (span.label === "secret" && span.confidence > 0.9) abort();
+  }
+}
+for await (const span of session.finish()) handle(span);
+```
 
-The official SDK is Python. `transformers.js` runs it on WebGPU but fails on CPU — ORT-Web's WASM bundle is missing `GatherBlockQuantized` and `MatMulNBits` kernels. **textsift is the only package that runs this model end-to-end client-side on both GPU and CPU**, with a single public API.
+Batch inputs, custom markers, per-category enabling — see the [API reference](https://teamchong.github.io/textsift/api/).
 
-## Performance
+## Measured numbers (M3 Pro, Chromium 147)
 
-M-series MacBook / Chromium 147, median of 5 runs after 2 warmups:
+Forward-pass latency, median of 5 runs:
 
-| | tjs (WebGPU) | **textsift (WebGPU)** | speedup |
+| Input length | textsift (WebGPU) | textsift (WASM ST) | tjs (WebGPU default) |
 |---|---:|---:|---:|
-| Short input | 29 ms | **7 ms** | **4.1×** |
-| Medium input | 39 ms | **12 ms** | **3.2×** |
-| Long input | 57 ms | **25 ms** | **2.3×** |
-| Second-visit warmup | 11.2 s | **1.1 s** | **10×** |
-
-Cold-start parity on first visit (both download ~770 MB from HF CDN); repeat visits hit our OPFS cache.
+| ~12 tokens | 7 ms | 66 ms | 29 ms |
+| ~25 tokens | 12 ms | 158 ms | 39 ms |
+| ~80 tokens | 25 ms | 342 ms | 57 ms |
 
-## Backends
+Cold start (defaults — both packages can be configured otherwise):
 
-Three interchangeable engines behind one API. `textsift-core` ships the first two; `textsift` adds the third for compatibility with WebGPU-less and SIMD-less runtimes.
+| | First visit | Second visit |
+|---|---:|---:|
+| tjs (Cache API default) | 13.3 s | 11.2 s |
+| textsift (OPFS default) | 14.1 s | 1.1 s |
 
-| Backend | Engine | Where |
-|---|---|---|
-| `webgpu` | Hand-tuned WGSL compute shaders | Modern Chromium / Safari / Firefox with WebGPU + `shader-f16` |
-| `wasm` | Zig + SIMD128, multi-thread when COOP/COEP set | Universal fallback. Only working CPU path for this model |
-| `auto` (transformers.js) | ORT-Web (umbrella `textsift` only) | Final fallback when neither path is viable |
-
-```ts
-const gpu = await PrivacyFilter.create({ backend: "webgpu" });   // fastest
-const wasm = await PrivacyFilter.create({ backend: "wasm" });    // universal
-const auto = await PrivacyFilter.create();                        // pick best
-```
+The second-visit gap is a default-configuration comparison, not an architectural ceiling. transformers.js with a custom OPFS cache plugged in can match textsift on second-visit warmup. The factual claim is "textsift caches large models persistently with no configuration", not "tjs can't cache".
 
-All paths produce byte-identical spans on the same input.
+These will look different on your hardware. They're a snapshot.
 
-## Repo layout (monorepo)
+## Repo layout (npm workspaces monorepo)
 
 ```
 packages/
@@ -95,9 +90,8 @@ packages/
     src/c/           ← FMA shim for relaxed_simd
   textsift/          ← umbrella: depends on textsift-core + @huggingface/transformers
     src/             ← thin wrapper, transformers.js fallback backend
-docs-site/           ← Astro + Starlight docs site (textsift.teamchong.github.io)
-tests/browser/       ← Playwright parity + benchmark tests
-docs/                ← engineering notes (roadmap, benchmarks)
+docs-site/           ← Astro + Starlight docs site
+tests/browser/       ← Playwright tests, including tokenizer-conformance + stream
 ```
 
 ## Development
@@ -106,16 +100,16 @@ docs/                ← engineering notes (roadmap, benchmarks)
 npm install                # workspace bootstrap
 npm run build              # builds both packages (zig → wasm, bundle, .d.ts)
 npm run typecheck          # strict, noUncheckedIndexedAccess on
-npm run test               # playwright browser tests
+npm run test               # all playwright tests
 ```
 
-The tokenizer-conformance test (`tests/browser/tokenizer-conformance.spec.ts`) verifies the native BPE tokenizer produces byte-for-byte identical token-id sequences to AutoTokenizer across a 46-case corpus (English, Unicode, code, edge whitespace, special tokens).
+The tokenizer-conformance test (`tests/browser/tokenizer-conformance.spec.ts`) verifies the native BPE tokenizer produces token-id sequences identical to AutoTokenizer across 46 cases (English, Unicode, code, edge whitespace, special tokens). The stream test (`tests/browser/stream.spec.ts`) verifies streaming detection yields the same spans as a single batch `detect()` call.
 
 ## Caveats
 
-`openai/privacy-filter` is a detection aid, **not an anonymization guarantee**. English-first (Japanese ~88% F1, other languages untested). Short text under-contextualizes.
+`openai/privacy-filter` is a detection aid, not an anonymization guarantee. English-first (Japanese ~88% F1, other languages untested). Short text under-contextualizes.
 
-See the [caveats page](https://teamchong.github.io/textsift/caveats/) and OpenAI's [model card](https://cdn.openai.com/pdf/c66281ed-b638-456a-8ce1-97e9f5264a90/OpenAI-Privacy-Filter-Model-Card.pdf) before treating redacted output as compliance-safe.
+Read the [caveats page](https://teamchong.github.io/textsift/caveats/) and OpenAI's [model card](https://cdn.openai.com/pdf/c66281ed-b638-456a-8ce1-97e9f5264a90/OpenAI-Privacy-Filter-Model-Card.pdf) before treating output as compliance-safe.
 
 ## License

@@ -10,8 +10,9 @@ description: How textsift turns text into redacted output, from tokenizer to spa
     │
     ▼
  ┌─────────────────────┐
- │ Tokenizer           │   @huggingface/transformers AutoTokenizer
- │   (shared JS)       │   + byte-level BPE reverse map for offsets
+ │ Tokenizer           │   Native o200k-style BPE in textsift-core
+ │   (shared JS)       │   (pretokenizer regex + byte-level encoder
+ │                     │    + BPE merge loop + special-token map)
  └──────────┬──────────┘
             │ tokenIds, attentionMask, tokenToCharOffset
             ▼
@@ -22,9 +23,9 @@ description: How textsift turns text into redacted output, from tokenizer to spa
             ▼
  ┌─────────────────────┐
  │ Backend.forward()   │   One of:
- │                     │     transformers.js (ORT-Web WebGPU)
- │                     │     webgpu (our WGSL)
- │                     │     wasm   (our Zig + SIMD128)
+ │                     │     webgpu (custom WGSL)            ← textsift-core
+ │                     │     wasm   (custom Zig + SIMD128)   ← textsift-core
+ │                     │     transformers-js (ORT-Web)       ← umbrella `textsift` only
  └──────────┬──────────┘
             │ Float32Array [T, 33]  (background + 8 labels × BIOES)
             ▼
@@ -73,28 +74,28 @@ We read OpenAI's upstream `onnx/model_q4f16.onnx` + `.onnx_data` directly. No co
 
 ## Backend internals
 
-### transformers.js (Stage 0)
+### Zig + WASM (in `textsift-core`)
 
-Thin wrapper around `AutoModelForTokenClassification`. We bypass `pipeline('token-classification')` because it runs its own argmax / BIOES decode; we need raw logits.
+Hand-written kernels in `packages/textsift-core/src/zig/wasm_exports.zig`, compiled with `-target wasm32-freestanding -mcpu=generic+simd128+relaxed_simd -O ReleaseFast`. The output `textsift.wasm` is ~92 KB, inlined into the JS bundle as base64.
 
-### Zig + WASM (Stage 1)
+Kernels: embed lookup, RMSNorm (incl. fused add+rms+widen variant), int4 matmul (three X×Out type variants), RoPE, banded attention with sinks, swiGLU with clamp, QMoE dispatch, scatter-add, cast, softmax, top-K. Multi-thread version (`textsift-mt.wasm`) builds with `+atomics+bulk_memory` for SAB-backed worker pools.
 
-Hand-written kernels in `src/zig/wasm_exports.zig`, compiled with `-target wasm32-freestanding -mcpu=generic+simd128+relaxed_simd -O ReleaseFast`. `dist/textsift.wasm` is ~25 KB, inlined into the JS bundle.
+### WGSL (in `textsift-core`)
 
-Kernels: embed lookup, RMSNorm, int4 matmul (three X×Out type variants), RoPE, banded attention with sinks, swiGLU with clamp, QMoE dispatch, scatter-add, cast, softmax, topK.
+14 compute shaders in `packages/textsift-core/src/js/backends/webgpu.ts`. Highlights:
 
-### WGSL (Stage 2)
-
-14 compute shaders in `src/js/backends/webgpu.ts`. Highlights:
-
-- `MATMUL_INT4_FP16_F16`: fp16 input → fp16 output, with **8-nibble/word unpack** (one u32 load dispatches 8 FMAs). Used for Q/K/V/O projections.
+- `MATMUL_INT4_FP16_F16`: fp16 input → fp16 output with 8-nibble/word unpack (one u32 load dispatches 8 FMAs). Used for Q/K/V/O projections.
 - `BANDED_ATTENTION`: one workgroup per `(t_query, h_query)`, 64 threads sharing scores/softmax scratch in workgroup memory, one thread per head_dim lane for AV combine.
 - `QMOE_GATE_UP` + `SWIGLU_CLAMP` + `QMOE_DOWN_SCATTER`: token-major MoE (one workgroup per `(token, k_pick)`), atomic CAS on f32-as-u32 bits for the scatter-add. No CPU readback needed.
 
+### transformers.js fallback (in umbrella `textsift` only)
+
+Thin wrapper around `AutoModelForTokenClassification`. We bypass `pipeline('token-classification')` because it runs its own argmax / BIOES decode; we need raw logits. Used by the umbrella's auto resolver only when both WebGPU and SIMD-WASM are unavailable.
+
 ## Design invariants
 
-1. **Public API is narrow** — only `PrivacyFilter.create`, `redact`, `detect`, `redactBatch`, `dispose`, and the 7 exported types.
-2. **Backends are interchangeable** — all three produce byte-identical spans.
-3. **Weights live outside the npm bundle** — package ships ~1.8 MB of JS; model is fetched on first use and persisted via OPFS.
-4. **No server-side fallback** — if inference can't run locally, we error out. We don't silently call home.
+1. **Public API is narrow** — `PrivacyFilter.create`, `redact`, `detect`, `redactBatch`, `startStream`, `dispose`, plus the exported types.
+2. **Backends are interchangeable** — all backends produce byte-identical spans on the same input.
+3. **Weights live outside the npm bundle** — `textsift-core` ships 76 KB gzipped of JS + a 90 KB WASM module. The 770 MB model is fetched on first use and persisted via OPFS by default.
+4. **No server-side fallback** — if inference can't run locally, an error is thrown. The library does not call any server.
 5. **Apache 2.0** — matches the upstream model.

@@ -3,56 +3,64 @@ title: Backends
 description: Three execution engines behind the same public API.
 ---
 
-`textsift` ships three interchangeable backends. The public API (`PrivacyFilter.create`, `redact`, `detect`) is identical across all three — the backend is an implementation detail picked at construction.
+textsift ships up to three interchangeable backends. The public API (`PrivacyFilter.create`, `redact`, `detect`) is identical across all of them — the backend is an implementation detail picked at construction.
 
-| Backend | Device | Compute | When to use |
-|---|---|---|---|
-| `auto` (default) | WebGPU via ORT-Web; CPU in Node | transformers.js | Drop-in, works everywhere |
-| `webgpu` | WebGPU with `shader-f16` | Hand-tuned WGSL | **Fastest.** Modern Chromium, recent Safari, Firefox with WebGPU enabled |
-| `wasm` | CPU | Zig + SIMD128 | Universal fallback. Only working CPU path for this model |
+| Backend | Where | Compute |
+|---|---|---|
+| `webgpu` | `textsift-core` and `textsift` | Custom WGSL kernels (int4 matmul, banded attention, sparse MoE). Requires `shader-f16`. |
+| `wasm` | `textsift-core` and `textsift` | Custom Zig + SIMD128 → WASM. Multi-thread when COOP/COEP headers set. |
+| `auto` (transformers.js fallback) | `textsift` only | ORT-Web with WebGPU EP. Used by the umbrella's auto path on browsers without WebGPU and without SIMD-WASM. |
 
 ## Backend decision tree
 
 ```
- ┌──────────────────────────┐
- │ Does target have WebGPU? │
- │   (navigator.gpu +       │
- │    shader-f16 feature)   │
- └────────────┬─────────────┘
+ PrivacyFilter.create({ backend?: ... })
               │
-       ┌──────┴──────┐
-       │             │
-      yes            no
-       │             │
-       ▼             ▼
- backend:"webgpu"   backend:"wasm"
- (fastest)          (only CPU option)
+              ▼
+ ┌────────────────────────────────────────┐
+ │ explicit backend ?                     │
+ │   "webgpu" → use it                    │
+ │   "wasm"   → use it                    │
+ └────────────┬───────────────────────────┘
+              │ (no explicit backend or "auto")
+              ▼
+ ┌────────────────────────────────────────┐
+ │ navigator.gpu + shader-f16 available ? │
+ └────────────┬───────────────────────────┘
+       yes    │    no
+       ▼            ▼
+   backend:"webgpu"   backend:"wasm"
+                          ↑
+            in `textsift-core` we stop here
+            in `textsift` (umbrella), if WASM
+            also can't run, fall back to
+            transformers.js
 ```
 
 ## Compatibility
 
 | | Chromium 147 | Firefox 129 | Safari 19 | Node 22 |
 |---|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
-| `auto` (tjs + WebGPU) | ✅ | ✅ with `dom.webgpu.enabled=true` | ✅ | ⚠️ CPU-only via ORT-Node |
-| `webgpu` | ✅ | ⚠️ shader-f16 preview | ⚠️ shader-f16 preview | ❌ |
+| `webgpu` | ✅ | ⚠️ `shader-f16` preview | ⚠️ `shader-f16` preview | ❌ |
 | `wasm` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
+| `auto` (`textsift` only, falls back to transformers.js) | ✅ | ✅ if `dom.webgpu.enabled=true` | ✅ | ⚠️ via ORT-Node |
 
-Any browser that can run `model_q4f16.onnx` at all can run `backend: "auto"`. For CPU-only environments, only `backend: "wasm"` works — transformers.js fails session creation because ORT-Web's WASM bundle has no `GatherBlockQuantized` or `MatMulNBits` kernel.
+`backend: "wasm"` is the only currently-working CPU path for `model_q4f16.onnx`. transformers.js with `device: "wasm"` fails session creation because ORT-Web's WASM bundle has no implementation for the `GatherBlockQuantized` and `MatMulNBits` ONNX contrib ops the int4 export uses.
 
 ## Output parity
 
-All three backends produce **byte-identical spans** on the same input (cross-backend conformance test: `tests/browser/smoke.spec.ts` + `webgpu-smoke.spec.ts`). Logit magnitudes may drift by up to 0.2 RMS due to fp16 rounding differences across 8 layers, but argmax (and hence Viterbi + span decode) is bit-exact.
+All backends produce **byte-identical spans** on the same input (`tests/browser/smoke.spec.ts` + `webgpu-smoke.spec.ts`). Logit magnitudes drift by up to ~0.2 RMS due to fp16 rounding differences accumulating across 8 transformer layers, but argmax (and hence Viterbi + span decode) is preserved.
 
 ## Switching backends at runtime
 
-You can't — a `PrivacyFilter` instance is bound to one backend for its lifetime. To switch, dispose and recreate:
+A `PrivacyFilter` instance is bound to one backend for its lifetime. To switch, dispose and recreate:
 
 ```ts
-const wasmFilter = await PrivacyFilter.create({ backend: "wasm" });
-await wasmFilter.redact(text);
-wasmFilter.dispose();
+const wasm = await PrivacyFilter.create({ backend: "wasm" });
+await wasm.redact(text);
+wasm.dispose();
 
-const gpuFilter = await PrivacyFilter.create({ backend: "webgpu" });
+const gpu = await PrivacyFilter.create({ backend: "webgpu" });
 ```
 
 Both share the same OPFS cache for the 770 MB model — switching is a ~1.1-second warmup, not a full re-download.
@@ -61,8 +69,8 @@ Both share the same OPFS cache for the 770 MB model — switching is a ~1.1-seco
 
 | Backend | Resident memory during inference |
 |---|---|
-| `auto` (tjs WebGPU) | ~870 MB JS heap + ~1 GB GPU |
-| `webgpu` | ~30 MB JS heap + ~800 MB GPU |
+| `webgpu` | ~30 MB JS heap + ~800 MB GPU buffers |
 | `wasm` | ~800 MB WASM linear memory |
+| `auto` (transformers.js path) | ~870 MB JS heap + ~1 GB GPU |
 
-`webgpu` is the most memory-efficient since weights live on-device and JS holds only kernel dispatch state.
+`webgpu` is the most memory-efficient since weights live in GPU buffers and JS holds only kernel-dispatch state.

@@ -1,31 +1,35 @@
 ---
 title: Benchmarks
-description: Measured numbers vs transformers.js on identical inputs.
+description: Measured numbers comparing the three backends.
 ---
 
-All numbers measured on an M-series MacBook, Chromium 147 with `--enable-unsafe-webgpu --use-angle=metal`. Median of 5 samples after 2 warmup iterations. Tests in `tests/browser/bench.spec.ts`.
+All numbers measured on an M3 Pro MacBook, Chromium 147 with `--enable-unsafe-webgpu --use-angle=metal`. Median of 5 samples after 2 warmup iterations. Reproduce with the test in `tests/browser/bench.spec.ts`.
+
+These numbers will look different on your hardware. They're a snapshot, not a guarantee.
 
 ## Per-forward latency
 
-| Input length | tjs (WebGPU) | **textsift (WebGPU)** | speedup | textsift (WASM) |
-|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
-| Short (~12 tokens) | 29 ms | **7 ms** | **4.1×** | 66 ms |
-| Medium (~25 tokens) | 39 ms | **12 ms** | **3.2×** | 158 ms |
-| Long (~80 tokens) | 57 ms | **25 ms** | **2.3×** | 342 ms |
+| Input length | textsift (WebGPU) | textsift (WASM ST) | tjs (WebGPU default) |
+|---|---:|---:|---:|
+| ~12 tokens | 7 ms | 66 ms | 29 ms |
+| ~25 tokens | 12 ms | 158 ms | 39 ms |
+| ~80 tokens | 25 ms | 342 ms | 57 ms |
 
-Linear scaling with T — dispatch overhead is negligible at this model size, the WebGPU path is compute-bound and well-utilized.
+Both WebGPU paths are GPU-bound at this model size; the difference reflects the kernels (textsift's hand-tuned int4 matmul / banded attention vs ORT-Web's int4 implementation via WebGPU contrib ops). The WASM single-thread number jumps to ~94 ms at 32 tokens with multi-threading enabled (COOP/COEP headers + 6 worker threads).
 
 ## Cold start
 
-| | First visit (HF CDN download) | Second visit (cache warm) |
+This is a default-configuration comparison, not an architectural ceiling.
+
+| | First visit | Second visit |
 |---|---:|---:|
-| tjs | 13.3 s | 11.2 s |
-| textsift (WASM) | 14.1 s | 1.8 s |
-| **textsift (WebGPU)** | 14.1 s ¹ | **1.1 s** |
+| tjs (Cache API default) | 13.3 s | 11.2 s |
+| textsift WASM (OPFS default) | 14.1 s | 1.8 s |
+| textsift WebGPU (OPFS default) | 14.1 s | 1.1 s |
 
-¹ First visit is download-bound (~770 MB from HuggingFace CDN). All backends take similar time on the initial download; the difference is on repeat visits.
+First visit is download-bound (~770 MB from HuggingFace CDN); the time goes to bytes, not code.
 
-**Second-visit cold start — textsift webgpu is 10× faster than transformers.js.** The win comes from OPFS-backed caching: Cache API can't hold the 770 MB `.onnx_data` blob (quota rejects it), so we persist via OPFS where Chromium allocates ~60% of free disk per origin and Safari gives 1 GB.
+Second visit: textsift writes the 770 MB model to OPFS by default. transformers.js's default cache is the Cache API, which silently rejects payloads this large — so its uncached path runs every visit. A transformers.js user who plugs in a custom OPFS-backed cache can match textsift's second-visit warmup. The factual claim is **"textsift caches large models persistently with no configuration"**, not "textsift can cache and tjs can't".
 
 ## Warmup breakdown (cache hit)
 
@@ -42,9 +46,18 @@ From `tests/browser/webgpu-coldstart.spec.ts`, second warmup:
 
 ## Accuracy
 
-On the model's own benchmark (PII-Masking-300k), `openai/privacy-filter` reports **F1 96%** (state-of-the-art). All three textsift backends produce byte-identical span output (`tests/browser/smoke.spec.ts`, `webgpu-smoke.spec.ts`), so the downstream accuracy is the model's.
+`openai/privacy-filter` reports F1 96% on PII-Masking-300k in OpenAI's own evaluation. textsift doesn't change the model — all three backends produce byte-identical span output on the same input (`tests/browser/smoke.spec.ts`, `webgpu-smoke.spec.ts`). The detection accuracy is the model's, not ours.
+
+The forward-parity test (`tests/browser/webgpu-forward.spec.ts`) confirms argmax agrees on 32/32 tokens of a deterministic input, with logit RMS drift of 0.18 vs the WASM reference — fp16 rounding compounding across 8 transformer layers. Argmax (and downstream Viterbi + span output) is preserved.
+
+## Bundle size
+
+| Package | Minified | Gzipped (over the wire) |

+Plus a 90 KB `.wasm` file loaded async (not in the JS bundle).

-## Why
-
-OpenAI released [openai/privacy-filter](https://huggingface.co/openai/privacy-filter) on 2026-04-20 — a 1.5B-parameter MoE token classifier (50M active) for PII detection, Apache 2.0. It reaches **96-97% F1 on PII-Masking-300k** and runs on a laptop.
-
-The gap: the official SDK is Python. transformers.js runs it on WebGPU but has no CPU fallback. **`textsift` is the only package that runs this model end-to-end in the browser with both WebGPU and CPU paths.**
-
-## Features
-
-<CardGrid>
-  <Card title="Fully client-side" icon="seti:lock">
-    No backend. No network inference calls. PII text stays on the user's device.
-  </Card>
-  <Card title="Three backends" icon="seti:config">
-    WebGPU (hand-tuned WGSL, fastest), WASM (Zig + SIMD128, universal), or transformers.js (baseline). One public API.
-  </Card>
-  <Card title="Faster than tjs" icon="rocket">
-    2.3–4× faster per forward than transformers.js; 10× faster on repeat-visit cold start.
-  </Card>
-  <Card title="OPFS cache" icon="document">
-    770 MB model downloaded once, persisted across visits. Second launch: 1.1 seconds.
-  </Card>
-  <Card title="Byte-exact decoding" icon="approve-check">
-    Viterbi CRF over BIOES, matching OpenAI's reference decoding.

-## Two packages, your choice
+## What this is
+
+A npm package that runs [openai/privacy-filter](https://huggingface.co/openai/privacy-filter) — a 1.5B / 50M-active MoE bidirectional token classifier for PII detection — entirely on the user's device. WebGPU when available, WASM (Zig + SIMD128) otherwise. Apache 2.0.
+
+The model is OpenAI's; the value of this package is packaging:
+
+- A native o200k-style BPE tokenizer in pure TypeScript (no `@huggingface/transformers` dep on the lean install).
+- Two custom backends — WGSL kernels for WebGPU and Zig-compiled SIMD WASM — that produce the same logits as transformers.js's WebGPU path on the same input.
+- A WebAssembly path that works at all on this model: ORT-Web's WASM EP (which transformers.js uses under the hood) is missing the `MatMulNBits` and `GatherBlockQuantized` kernels the int4 export needs, so `transformers.js` with `device: "wasm"` fails session creation. textsift's WASM backend is a from-scratch implementation that doesn't go through ORT-Web.
+- Persistent OPFS caching of the 770 MB model weights, configured by default. Same data the model needs anyway, just stored on the user's disk so the second visit doesn't re-download. transformers.js can be configured to do this too — by default it uses the Cache API which silently rejects payloads this big.
+- An incremental `startStream()` API for AI-proxy / LLM-output-filtering use cases. Streams text, yields spans as they become detectable, O(N) total work over a stream of N tokens.
+
+## Two packages
 
 ```bash
-npm install textsift-core   # ~76 KB gzipped, no transformers.js dep
-npm install textsift        # ~228 KB gzipped, adds auto fallback
+npm install textsift-core   # 76 KB gzipped, no runtime deps
+npm install textsift        # 226 KB gzipped, adds a transformers.js fallback backend
 ```
 
-Both expose the same `PrivacyFilter` API. The umbrella `textsift` package adds a transformers.js fallback path for runtimes without WebGPU or SIMD-capable WASM. Most apps want `textsift-core` — it's leaner and ships with a native o200k-style BPE tokenizer (no transformers.js dep at all).
+Both expose the same `PrivacyFilter` API. Pick the umbrella `textsift` if you need the transformers.js fallback (typically: browsers without WebGPU and without cross-origin-isolation for SIMD-WASM). Pick `textsift-core` if you don't.

-## How it compares
+## Measured numbers
 
-| | tjs (WebGPU) | **textsift (WebGPU)** | textsift (WASM) |
+M3 Pro MacBook, Chromium 147, median of 5 forward passes after 2 warmups. Reproduce with `npx playwright test bench.spec.ts`.
+
+| Input length | textsift (WebGPU) | textsift (WASM ST) | tjs (WebGPU default) |
 |---|---:|---:|---:|
-| Forward — short | 29 ms | **7 ms** | 66 ms |
-| Forward — medium | 39 ms | **12 ms** | 158 ms |
-| Forward — long | 57 ms | **25 ms** | 342 ms |
-| Second-visit warmup | 11.2 s | **1.1 s** | 1.8 s |
+| ~12 tokens | 7 ms | 66 ms | 29 ms |
+| ~25 tokens | 12 ms | 158 ms | 39 ms |
+| ~80 tokens | 25 ms | 342 ms | 57 ms |
+| Second-visit warmup ¹ | 1.1 s | 1.8 s | 11.2 s |
+
+¹ With OPFS caching configured (textsift default). transformers.js's default Cache API rejects 770 MB; if you wire OPFS into transformers.js manually, its second-visit warmup is comparable to textsift's.
+
+The forward-pass numbers reflect kernel-level differences (custom int4 WGSL vs ORT-Web's int4 WGSL via WebGPU contrib ops). They don't depend on caching configuration.
+
+## CPU-only environments
+
+`textsift` is the only currently-working path for `model_q4f16.onnx` outside of WebGPU. transformers.js's WASM EP can't load this model (missing `MatMulNBits` / `GatherBlockQuantized`); textsift's WASM backend is a separate implementation that runs the int4 matmul + GQA + sparse MoE in Zig-compiled SIMD128. Browsers without WebGPU, server-side Node, edge workers — all work.
 
-*M-series MacBook, Chromium 147, median of 5 runs after 2 warmups.*
+## Caveats
 
-For **CPU-only environments** (corporate Chrome without WebGPU, Firefox with `dom.webgpu.enabled=false`, server-side Node), textsift is the only working path — transformers.js fails session creation on this model's WASM device due to missing ORT-Web kernels (`GatherBlockQuantized`, `MatMulNBits`).
+`openai/privacy-filter` is a detection aid, not an anonymization guarantee. Read the [caveats page](/textsift/caveats/) and OpenAI's [model card](https://cdn.openai.com/pdf/c66281ed-b638-456a-8ce1-97e9f5264a90/OpenAI-Privacy-Filter-Model-Card.pdf) before treating output as compliance-safe.

-## When to pick `textsift-core` vs `textsift`
+## When `textsift-core` vs `textsift`
+
+`textsift-core` ships only the custom backends. If you want a transformers.js fallback for runtimes without WebGPU and without SIMD-capable WASM, install [`textsift`](https://www.npmjs.com/package/textsift) instead — same API, ~226 KB gzipped.
 
 | Scenario | Pick |
 |---|---|
-| Browser app with modern Chromium / Safari 18+ users | **`textsift-core`** |
-| Server-side Node 20+ with optional WebGPU | **`textsift-core`** |
-| Edge worker / Cloudflare Workers / Vercel Edge | **`textsift-core`** |
-| AI proxy that runs everywhere and needs minimum bundle | **`textsift-core`** |
-| Browser app that needs to support Firefox without `dom.webgpu.enabled` | [`textsift`](https://www.npmjs.com/package/textsift) |
-| Drop-in replacement for an existing transformers.js setup | [`textsift`](https://www.npmjs.com/package/textsift) |
+| Browser app on modern Chromium / Safari 18+ | `textsift-core` |
+| Server-side Node 20+ | `textsift-core` |
+| Edge worker, Cloudflare Workers, Vercel Edge | `textsift-core` |
+| AI proxy that needs to ship everywhere | `textsift-core` |
+| Browser that may not have WebGPU OR cross-origin-isolation for SIMD-WASM | `textsift` |
+| You're already using transformers.js elsewhere and want a drop-in replacement | `textsift` |

+Same `PrivacyFilter` API as `textsift-core` — every export from core is re-exported here, plus `TransformersJsBackend` for callers who want to instantiate the fallback directly.

+## When `textsift` vs `textsift-core`
 
 | Scenario | Pick |
 |---|---|
-| You don't know what backend your users have | **`textsift`** |
-| You want to support Firefox without `dom.webgpu.enabled` | **`textsift`** |
-| You want a drop-in replacement for transformers.js | **`textsift`** |
-| Browser app on modern Chromium / Safari 18+ | [`textsift-core`](https://www.npmjs.com/package/textsift-core) (3× smaller) |
-| Server-side Node 20+ | [`textsift-core`](https://www.npmjs.com/package/textsift-core) |
+| Browser that may not have WebGPU or COOP/COEP cross-origin-isolation | `textsift` |
+| Already using transformers.js elsewhere, want a drop-in replacement | `textsift` |
+| Browser app on modern Chromium / Safari 18+ where WebGPU is available | [`textsift-core`](https://www.npmjs.com/package/textsift-core) (3× smaller) |
+| Node 20+ server-side | [`textsift-core`](https://www.npmjs.com/package/textsift-core) |
 
 ## How auto-fallback works
 
 `PrivacyFilter.create()` with no `backend` option picks:
 
-1. **WebGPU** (custom WGSL kernels) — if a WebGPU adapter with `shader-f16` is available
-2. **WASM** (custom Zig + SIMD128, multi-thread when COOP/COEP set) — otherwise
-3. **transformers.js** (this package's contribution) — final fallback for browsers without WebGPU
+1. WebGPU (custom WGSL kernels) if a WebGPU adapter with `shader-f16` is available, OR
+2. WASM (custom Zig + SIMD128) if SharedArrayBuffer is available (cross-origin isolation), OR
+3. transformers.js — this package's contribution — as the final fallback.
 
-Force a specific backend with `{ backend: "webgpu" | "wasm" | "auto" }`.
+Force a specific path with `{ backend: "webgpu" | "wasm" }`.
 
 ## Public API
 
-Identical to [`textsift-core`](https://www.npmjs.com/package/textsift-core) — every export is re-exported here, plus `TransformersJsBackend` for callers who want to instantiate it directly.
+Identical to `textsift-core` plus:
+
+```ts
+import { TransformersJsBackend } from "textsift";
+// for callers who want to instantiate the fallback directly
+```
